data.title: Netflix: Time To Book Profits After Post Earnings Rally
data.description:
Following up on my earlier "Hold" thesis on Netflix(NASDAQ:NFLX), I have a "Sell" recommendation now after a rally in prices post Q1 2025 results. I believe the rally does not address a lot of concerns I had at the time of writing, while returning ~22% from the time of my thesis. This is an excellent opportunity to book profits and wait for a lower re-entry point. I am not recommending shorting the stock for two reasons. One NFLX may still go up even further; there are no price action indicators vindicating my "Sell" thesis yet. Two, I remain convinced about the health of Netflix overall and in the long term. The current "Sell" call is to take advantage of transient mispricing and evaluate re-entry at much lower levels, enhancing long-term returns. This approach is particularly important in the setting of growth concerns. I will wait for growth signals to mature before re-entering.

Why Netflix Rallied — Ad Growth could be a Game Changer but Takes Time

The positive management guidance on Q2 2025 and indications on doubling ad revenues in 2025 were positive from the Q1 2025 earnings report. Operating income also saw a healthy 27% YoY growth. However, management guidance was unchanged for 2025 overall. Management also talked about how the ad revenues are not significant enough compared to subscriptions yet, hence its doubling makes a strategic impact on valuations, not the topline or margins in any meaningful way in the short term.

The growth in ad revenues is encouraging for the long term, no doubt. However, a mature ad revenue indicator, YouTube ads revenues, is currently clocking ~$9B. When NTFLX reaches that level of ad maturity, it should increase topline by over 80% (assuming no growth in subscription revenue at ~$40b and no ad revenue now, both assumptions favoring the ad impact estimate). The impact on margins is likely to be even higher once the ad tech capex and capability stabilize. In such scenarios, the current high valuations get justified to a large extent. In my previous analysis, I had mentioned Netflix's valuation expects potential to at least double earnings. That looks possible.

Only that the valuations have again moved north, and the asking rate of earnings is higher than the doubling potential we just saw. Keep in mind, the doubling potential is a very optimistic scenario and reaching there is not beyond Netflix, but will take at least a couple of years. In this period, NFLX will have to work through capability building and R&D tugging at its cash balances. And will see higher subscription on ad tiers, pulling down Average Revenue Per Membership (ARPM). For a few quarters, I expect the drop in revenues from dropping ARPM not be compensated by ad revenues. And as the ad capabilities and results mature, migration to higher tiers is not ruled out — cancelling out the ad growth expectations in favor of higher but not sufficient incremental subscription revenues.

Conclusion — bullish about the growth in ad revenues in the long haul. But there are assumptions in execution. This will take some time. Valuation gets some support due to this long-term support kicking in, but should see some interim contraction as NFLX goes through a lower growth period (due to lower ARPM and ad capability building) towards that evolved state. This thesis is further strengthened as I strongly believe, some part of the rally is attributable to investors moving towards perceived tariff and economic shockproof investments, creating a mini bubble in NFLX. I am mindful that the bubble could persist and NFLX may never correct meaningfully on its path to what I am calling the bullish evolved state. Hence, the advice is not to short NFLX, but rather to book profits. Even a time correction is an opportunity cost, and re-entries can be made later.

What Else has Developed

A key development, I view as a negative, is Netflix stopping its subscription detail data from Q1 2025. That data was a key performance indicator for years and provided good insight into analyzing Netflix's health. This removal of transparency definitely does not look like a positive development from a corporate transparency perspective. However, many companies, with their evolution, move to broader level metrics. This is just a short-term negative, but could be amplified by any further news of plateauing growth (as markets may suspect the move is designed to cover that).

I saw a drop in top-line growth levels in UCAN. The 9% YoY revenue growth in Q1 2025 is lower than the 15% YoY growth seen in Q4 2024. Of course, the growth levels in APAC compensate, but that is expected. I would have liked to see UCAN holding up a little better. Growth hinging on emerging markets is subject to increased competition and lower margins.

Signs of revenue growth cooling after the gains from the password sharing crackdown are visible in Q1 2025. That also remains a concern.

Book Profits

Overall, post the Q1 2025 results, share prices have gone up and pushed valuations even higher. The ad revenues are a great sign, but I still recommend a tactical "Sell" (book profits rather than a short), anticipating short-term headwinds and a better entry point to ride the long-term fundamentals.

data.title: Moat Index Finds Strength In Submarines And Semiconductors
data.description:
Despite April’s market volatility, the Moat Index found support from strong performers in defense and tech, showcasing the resilience of wide moat stocks amid trade tensions and recession fears.

In April, volatility in U.S. equity markets intensified, as the cautious investor sentiment and trade war fears that gripped March surged to new heights following President Trump’s “Liberation Day” on April 2, which unleashed sweeping tariffs and triggered sharp retaliatory measures, sending shockwaves through global markets and deepening economic uncertainty. Equities reacted violently to the disruption, with the S&P 500 plummeting more than 10% in the days following the tariff announcement, marking its worst week since the 2020 Covid crash. Pressures eventually eased after Trump announced a 90-day tariff pause on most nations, except China, providing relief to a market under severe strain. However, even with a notable rebound off intra-month lows, U.S. equities finished lower in April as investors continued to digest volatile trade tensions, renewed inflation uncertainty, and the increased threat of a potential recession.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) slipped 2.09% in April, trailing the cap-weighted S&P 500’s 0.68% decline yet edging ahead of the S&P 500 Equal-Weighted Index’s 2.29% loss. The Moat Index’s equally weighted construction, which spreads exposure more evenly and therefore reduces concentration in the market’s largest mega-cap names, proved a headwind this month. An overweight health care position, once a tailwind earlier in the year, also worked against the Index as fresh tariff threats on pharmaceutical imports pressured the sector. Together, these factors left the Moat Index behind the broad market for the month despite beneficial exposure from other market segments.

Down market-cap, the Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the “SMID Moat Index”) retreated 3.29% during the month, falling between the small-cap benchmark’s 4.19% drop and the mid-cap benchmark’s 2.25% decline, mirroring its blended size exposure. Smaller companies were hit harder as tariff threats heightened concerns that trade tensions could drag on domestic GDP growth, a dynamic to which they are naturally more sensitive than larger multinationals. Even with the setback, the SMID Moat Index still leads both the small- and mid-cap benchmarks on a year-to-date basis.

Stocks Slide in April on Volatile Trade Tension

Source: Morningstar. Data as of 4/30/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Please see index definitions and other important disclosures at the end of this content.

Moat Index April Highlights: Ships and Chips Sail Ahead

Sector positioning played a primary role in the Moat Index’s relative showing against the S&P 500 in April. A complete absence of energy stocks, a group that suffered the month’s steepest losses, added a tailwind, and a larger-than-benchmark stake in consumer staples and industrial names also helped. These benefits were, unfortunately, more than offset, however, by the Index’s heavier weight in health care, where threats of targeted tariffs, specifically toward pharmaceutical imports, dragged on the sector.

For the second straight month, wide-moat military shipbuilder Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) held the performance crown. After sidestepping March’s market turbulence, the stock steamed ahead in April on the strength of a solid first-quarter earnings beat. Momentum built further when management announced a new U.S. Navy contract for two Virginia-class submarines, coupled with fresh congressional funding to boost shipbuilding productivity. That powerful combination catapulted HII shares into double-digit gains and prompted Morningstar analyst Nicolas Owens to raise his fair-value estimate to $316 and reiterate his view that Huntington remains undervalued.

Also within the top contributors list for April is the newcomer semiconductor company Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), which just recently made its debut in the Moat Index at the March reconstitution. Cadence provides leading-edge electronic design automation tools and IP critical to the semiconductor chip design process. It benefits from switching costs and intangible assets and was upgraded to a wide moat rating from narrow in December 2024. It historically traded well above fair value until just earlier this year, allowing it to make the cut for the Moat Index. Shares of Cadence rallied 17 percent in April following positive earnings results and a steady growth trajectory amid macroeconomic uncertainty.

Other top contributors within the Moat Index during the month include the well-known aerospace and defense giant Boeing Co. (BA), global security products and solutions company Allegion (ALLE), and the semiconductor design software provider Synopsys (SNPS).

Detracting most this month were companies particularly impacted by tariff uncertainty, several belonging to the health care sector, including leading medical technology firm GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC), orthopedic implants provider Zimmer Biomet (ZBH), and drug manufacturer Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY). Footwear and apparel brand Nike (NKE) and the world's largest parcel delivery company, United Parcel Service (UPS), both heavily influenced by tariffs, were also notable detractors.

Source: Morningstar, April 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

SMID Moat Index April Highlights: Data Centers Keep Their Cool

The SMID Moat Index’s April performance benefited from favorable sector positioning relative to the mid-cap benchmark, with underweights in financials and real estate, two of the weaker-performing sectors, alongside a higher allocation to technology stocks, which provided a modest lift. However, those advantages were offset by stock-specific weakness, stemming from names that were disproportionately affected by the heightened tariff uncertainty.

Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT) defied April’s market volatility, gaining 18 percent only weeks after joining the SMID Moat Index in the March reconstitution. The surge followed a standout first-quarter report that delivered 25 percent year-over-year organic revenue growth and nearly 50 percent earnings-per-share expansion. Management also lifted full-year sales guidance by approximately three percentage points. The robust results reinforced investor confidence in Vertiv’s key role in supplying critical infrastructure equipment, primarily power management and cooling systems, to data centers increasingly focused on cloud computing and AI-driven workloads.

Morningstar assigns Vertiv a narrow economic moat, reflecting the durability of its Liebert brand, which maintains leading market positions in thermal management and power equipment, as well as customer relationships that frequently span more than two decades. Analyst Nicholas Lieb, CFA, maintains a $103 fair-value estimate, citing Vertiv’s pricing power and the essential nature of its products as valuable safeguards against potential tariff-related cost pressures arising from its manufacturing facilities in Mexico and component sourcing from China.

Other notable gainers in April included the automobile retailer AutoNation (AN), cloud-native cybersecurity software provider Zscaler (ZS), global security products and solutions company Allegion (ALLE), as well as respiratory care and medical device developer ResMed (RMD).

The biggest laggards were independent oil and gas producer Hess Corp. (HES), the largest domestic used-vehicle retailer CarMax (KMX), cruise line operator and travel service provider Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH), medical technology firm GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC), and protein sciences market leader Bio-Techne Corp. (TECH).

data.title: BigCommerce Holdings, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by BigCommerce Holdings, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Rockwell Automation Is Best Positioned For Any Market Environment
data.description:
Rockwell Automation, Inc. (NYSE:ROK) reported exceptionally strong Q2 ’25 financial results as management works to mitigate any financial risks resulting from tariff policies. With the majority of manufacturing being located in the U.S., Rockwell has significant flexibility in altering supply chains and materials sourcing, allowing for the firm to service U.S.-based customers through U.S. facilities and international customers through facilities in Mexico.

With Rockwell participating in multiple megatrends, including industrial automation and data center growth, I believe the firm is better positioned to realize significant operational leverage as macroeconomic uncertainty abates. Given the strong operational outlook, I am recommending ROK shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $338/share at 21.74x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Rockwell Automation Operations

Rockwell reported exceptionally strong Q2 ’25 results, with the organization’s book-to-bill ratio normalizing at 1:1. In the quarter, Rockwell received some pull forward in customer purchasing, but was relatively negligible when considering order conversion. In order to mitigate prebuying risk, Rockwell has placed limits on distributors and machine builders from stocking inventory beyond appropriate levels.

As a result of the risk mitigation efforts enacted in Q1 ’25, tariffs had a minimal impact on costs in Q2 ’25, resulting in exceptionally strong operating margins. The corporate operating margin expanded by 128bps in Q2 ’25 when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Margins for both intelligent devices and software & controls expanded in the quarter by 121bps and 444bps, respectively, driven by cost control measures management put in place. Rockwell realized roughly $155mm in cost savings throughout 1h25. Management is now expecting to exceed their $250mm cost savings target for eFY25 following the strong cost reductions put in place in 1h25.

Though year-over-year growth was challenged, the intelligent devices and software & control segments both grew sequentially, potentially signaling the beginning of the recovery.

Rockwell experienced some headwinds in its lifecycle services segment with certain projects being delayed in the automotive and energy industries as well as some deferrals of discretionary spend in digital services as a result of market uncertainty from the current tariff policies. The segment book-to-bill was above the corporate average at 1.07x, likely as a result of these delays. Despite these headwinds, annual recurring revenue [ARR] grew by 8% in the quarter.

In discrete industries, Rockwell saw strength in both its warehouse automation and semiconductor businesses. Accordingly, eCommerce and warehouse automation grew by 45% in North America and EMEA in Q2 ’25 when compared to the previous year’s quarter.

With the expectation of more manufacturing being reshored to the U.S., Rockwell is expanding its production of its OTTO autonomous mobile robots in the U.S. and Canada. These robots particularly cater to high-growth markets for Rockwell, including transportation and warehousing automation. This market is expected to grow at a 9.3% CAGR from 2024-2030, potentially providing a strong uplift in growth for Rockwell.

Another growth market may be in data centers. This includes its modular industrial data centers as well as its infrastructure-as-a-service [IaaS] for OT environments, which converge IT & OT to service industrial automation. Rockwell also supplies modular power distribution systems for data centers through its CUBIC business. As part of its growth strategy, Rockwell partnered with Cape Electrical Supply Integration, supplying products like its motor control centers for new power generation for the data center market. Data center power demand is expected to grow around 55GW to 84GW by 2027. Analysts at Goldman Sachs estimate that power demand from data centers could grow by as much as 165% by 2030 when compared to 2023 levels.

Tariff Risk

Rockwell initiated certain risk mitigation efforts in Q1 ’25 in order to minimize the impact on operations. Accordingly, cost exposure to Mexico, Canada, and China combined was less than 10% of revenue in FY24 with only $350mm in imports from Mexico into the U.S. Imports from China and Canada were each roughly $100mm. Mitigation efforts included price increases as well as adjustments to manufacturing locations. Given the firm’s manufacturing footprint in the U.S. and Mexico, management transitioned product manufacturing in each country to service U.S. customers in U.S.-based manufacturing facilities and international customers in Mexico-based manufacturing facilities, minimizing the impact of import duties. In all, tariffs weren’t expected to impact eFY25 financials.

In addition to this, Rockwell has been working to secure alternative sourcing of materials. In Q2 ’25, Rockwell realized $18mm in cost savings as a result of direct sourcing and an additional $20mm from manufacturing optimization. For e2h25, management is expecting $125mm in tariff cost exposure. Management will continue working through mitigation actions in order to offset these potential costs throughout the remainder of the fiscal year.

Rockwell Automation Financial Position

Management narrowed and increased their guidance for adjusted EPS for eFY25 on the back of strong operational performance in 1h25.

Despite sales growth in e2h25 expected to be offset by the decline realized in the first half of the fiscal year, margins have significantly improved with the notion of marginal improvements throughout the duration of the fiscal year.

For Q3 ’25, I’m forecasting Rockwell to generate $2b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.56/share. This should land Rockwell at a -2% top-line decline for eFY25 with a modest decline in adjusted EPS to $9.64/share.

Looking to the balance sheet, Rockwell had a modest working capital build in Q2 ’25 driven by an increase in receivables. Inventories declined modestly to 88 days on hand for a -5% sequential reduction. I suspect this is due in part from manufacturing migration as well as some customer pushing out deliveries. Nonetheless, Rockwell remains in a strong financial position, generating $171mm in free cash flow. For eFY25, I’m forecasting the organization to generate just over $1b in free cash flow as working capital is managed down.

Risks Related to Rockwell Automation

Bull Case

Rockwell set in motion its tariff mitigation strategy in Q1 ’25 that showed beneficial results in Q2 ’25, creating an uplift in margins despite the revenue headwinds. Though some order deliveries were pushed out as a result of the tariff uncertainty, management confirmed that cancellations were minimal, suggesting that revenue may accrue at a later date.

Looking at Rockwell as a long-term investment, the firm is well-positioned across a variety of industries that are heavily focusing on automation, including transportation & warehousing, data centers, manufacturing, and potentially the automotive industry if production is set to migrate into the U.S.

Bear Case

Macroeconomic uncertainty may persist as tariff policy adjusts, potentially further pushing out order deliveries, resulting in a decline in revenue generation. The ISM-PMI Manufacturing Index for April printed a contractionary environment in the U.S., primarily driven by uncertainty of tariff policy going forward.

The risk of a recession may also add pressure to growth for the remainder of 2025. Earlier in April, J. P. Morgan economists increased the probability of a recession for 2025 to 60%, up from their previous 40% risk. I do not place much weight in the Q1 ’25 GDP estimate given that the decline was likely driven by a pull forward in imports prior to the implementation of tariff policy.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

ROK shares currently trade at 20.38x EV/aEBITDA, a slight discount to my estimated midpoint of 23.29x. I believe this positions ROK shares well for investors seeking to start or build a position in ROK given the long-term growth trajectory across its respective markets.

In Q2 ’25, Rockwell repurchased 450k shares valued at $129mm, making YTD repurchasing $300mm as of Q2 ’25. Though the shares repurchased in 1h25 achieved management’s target for all of eFY25, there may be some additional buybacks in line as management views the “recent market pricing as an attractive opportunity to buy more Rockwell.” I don’t believe this is evidence enough to assume that more repurchases will occur; however, I do agree that ROK shares are relatively cheap and worth picking up at the current price levels.

From a tactical perspective, ROK shares appear to be moving in a bull trend. Given the recent price increase following Q2 ’25 earnings, shares may pull back to digest the new price level; however, I suspect the bullish trend may continue.

From a fundamental perspective, I believe shares should be priced at $338/share at 21.74x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA. Given the strong operational outlook, I am recommending ROK shares with a Strong Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: What The Smartest Canadian Investors Hold In Their Dividend Growth Portfolios
data.description:
Every year, Dividend Stocks Rock compiles a unique list: the most widely held stocks by its PRO members. With over 2,600 portfolios contributing to this list, it’s more than a popularity contest—it’s a reflection of what experienced, long-term dividend growth investors trust to deliver both income and resilience. This post spotlights three of Canada’s most-owned dividend stocks, offering insight into what seasoned investors value today and what DSR bull and bear cases are.

The U.S. companies will follow up next week.

Fortis (FTS:CA), (FTS) – The Boring Backbone of Stability Investors Count On

Fortis keeps on getting more love year after year. Fortis is a classic utility offering its customers transmission and distribution of electricity and natural gas. The company likes to remind investors that Fortis is virtually 100% regulated. It’s a symbol of stability and predictable cash flow.

Why it’s in the top 5

Fortis isn’t flashy. It’s reliable. It operates almost entirely regulated utilities across 18 jurisdictions.

What seasoned investors like

  • 50 years of uninterrupted dividend growth.

  • Recession-resistant and rate-regulated, it’s the poster child for “sleep-well-at-night” investing.

DSR Bull Case

Fortis is a top-tier regulated utility with operations across Canada, the U.S., and the Caribbean, generating 99% of its revenue from stable, government-approved sources. Its C$26 billion capital plan aims to grow its rate base by 6.5% annually through 2028, supporting steady earnings and dividend increases. With 50 consecutive years of dividend growth and growing investments in transmission and renewable energy, Fortis offers a rare blend of income reliability and long-term infrastructure-driven growth. It’s a classic “buy and hold” stock for conservative, income-focused investors.

DSR Bear Case

Despite its stability, Fortis faces headwinds from its capital-intensive business model and reliance on regulatory approvals. With one-third of its investment plan funded by debt, rising interest rates could pressure earnings and limit flexibility. Regulatory setbacks or environmental policy shifts may further strain growth. Currency fluctuations also pose a risk, as 60% of revenue is U.S.-based. In a competitive utility landscape, rivals with leaner operations or faster-growing assets could outpace Fortis in total returns.

Its portfolio role

Defensive core holding—ideal during volatile markets and for steady dividend growth.

Royal Bank (RY:CA), (RY) – Canada’s Financial Fortress with Global Reach

Royal Bank is the largest Canadian Bank by market cap and offers a wide variety of products and services. The business is separated into several segments, including Personal & Commercial (P&C), Capital Markets, Wealth Management, and Insurance.

Why it’s in the top 5

Royal Bank is a bedrock of stability, with diversification across personal banking, capital markets, and wealth management.

What seasoned investors like

  • Diversified revenue streams protect against housing market weakness.

  • Strong dividend growth track record and capital strength.

DSR Bull Case

Royal Bank of Canada stands as the country’s largest bank, supported by a well-diversified business model across banking, wealth management, insurance, and capital markets. Over half its revenue now comes from non-traditional banking sectors, offering stability and profitability beyond interest margins. Its international expansion adds resilience against domestic economic slowdowns, while strong brand recognition and regulatory protection create high barriers to entry. With steady earnings, growing fee-based revenue, and a reliable dividend history, RBC is a core holding for investors seeking both income and long-term growth.

DSR Bear Case

Despite its diversification, RBC remains vulnerable to macroeconomic pressures, particularly Canada’s housing market. Rising interest rates and economic uncertainty could lead to higher mortgage defaults and elevated credit losses. The bank has already increased provisions in anticipation of a downturn. Regulatory constraints limit growth flexibility, while a potential recession could dampen lending and capital markets activity. Competition from other major banks and global players also pressures RBC to continuously innovate just to maintain its position. These challenges may weigh on profitability and limit upside in the near term.

Its portfolio role

Anchor stock for both income and growth investors.

Enbridge (ENB:CA), (ENB) – Reliable Income from a Pipeline Powerhouse

Enbridge lost some love this year and was beaten out by Royal Bank. Some investors would be right to highlight the amazing year ENB had on the market. A part of that “bull run” is explained by its poor performance last year. Unfortunately, this is often the case for such a company: ENB stock price is known to go up and down through cycles. I bought it during one of those down cycles and was happy to sell it at a high price before the 2023 drop.

Why it’s one of the most held stocks

Enbridge combines reliable income with defensive characteristics, making it ideal for retirees and income investors.

What seasoned investors like

  • Enbridge operates one of North America’s largest pipeline systems, generating toll-like income from oil and gas transportation.

  • The company has increased its dividend for 29 consecutive years—a rare feat in the energy space.

  • Investors appreciate its strategic pivot toward natural gas and renewables, improving its long-term sustainability.

DSR Bull Case

Enbridge is a North American energy infrastructure giant, offering stable, inflation-resistant cash flow through long-term, take-or-pay contracts. Its Mainline system handles 70% of Canada’s crude exports, while recent acquisitions have expanded its footprint in natural gas transmission—now half its business. The company is targeting 7–9% annual EBITDA growth through 2026 and is investing in energy transition projects like renewable natural gas and offshore wind. With hard-to-replicate assets and a growing utility base, Enbridge remains a reliable income-generating powerhouse for long-term dividend investors.

DSR Bear Case

Enbridge’s aggressive growth strategy has led to a soaring debt load—reaching nearly $100 billion in 2024—which could strain future investments and dividend growth, especially amid rising interest rates and inflation. Regulatory and environmental hurdles remain a persistent threat, with pipeline projects often delayed or derailed. Increasing carbon taxes and operating costs in Canada may further erode margins. While Enbridge’s asset base is robust, its debt-heavy model puts it at a disadvantage compared to leaner midstream competitors with stronger balance sheets.

Its role in a portfolio

Enbridge is your premium income generator. It likely won’t deliver much capital growth, but it offers a steady, well-covered yield and solid inflation-beating income.

Final Thoughts: Seasoned Investors Choose Balance

DSR PRO members aren’t just picking high yielders or tech flyers. They’re selecting companies that combine resilience, income, and future potential. That’s a powerful takeaway for anyone building or reviewing a dividend portfolio.

Portfolio Role Recap Table

Stock Role in Portfolio Ideal For
Fortis Defensive Core Stability, retirement income
Royal Bank Balanced Anchor Growth + income blend
Enbridge Yield Generator Income-focused investors

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
